embeds.title: Python: The Documentary | An origin story
embeds.description:
This is the story of the world's most beloved programming language: Python. What began as a side project in Amsterdam during the 1990s became the software powering artificial intelligence, data science and some of the world’s biggest companies. But Python's future wasn't certain; at one point it almost disappeared.

embeds.title: The Free Transformer
embeds.description: We propose an extension of the decoder Transformer that conditions its generative process on random latent variables which are learned without supervision thanks to a variational procedure. Experimental evaluations show that allowing such a conditioning translates into substantial improvements on downstream tasks.

2.1 Naive Vector RAG — "The Librarian Who Only Reads Vibes"
Imagine a librarian whose only skill is sensing the mood of a book. You walk in and say "I need something about heartbreak" and she closes her eyes, feels the emotional energy of every book in the library, and hands you the 20 that feel most heartbreak-adjacent. She's pretty good at this.
Now you ask her "find me the book by an author named Dostoevsky." She closes her eyes again... and hands you books that feel deep and philosophical and Russian. Maybe one of them is actually Dostoevsky. Maybe not. She doesn't read names, she reads vibes.
That's dense vector search. It converts your query into a list of numbers (an embedding) that captures meaning, then finds the messages whose numbers are closest. It's great at understanding what you mean but terrible at caring about exact words.
In TealDR this means /lookup query: what did sidtheitguy say might return messages about sidtheitguy written by someone else, because the semantic meaning is similar. The exact username doesn't register as special — it's just another token in the vibe calculation.

2.2 Hybrid RAG — "Two Librarians, One Answer"
You hire a second librarian alongside the first. This one is the opposite — she doesn't understand meaning at all, but she has a photographic memory for exact words. You say "Dostoevsky" and she instantly pulls every book with that exact string on the cover. She can find sidtheitguy or Docker v2.1 or #deployments with perfect accuracy. But ask her for "something about existential dread" and she's lost — she only knows exact words, not concepts.
The smart move is using both librarians simultaneously and then merging their lists. That merging process is called Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). It works like this — if Librarian 1 (semantic) ranked a message #3 and Librarian 2 (keyword) ranked the same message #1, that message gets a high combined score because both agreed on it from different angles. Messages that only one librarian liked get a lower combined score.
The formula is simple: score = 1/(60 + rank_from_semantic) + 1/(60 + rank_from_keyword). The 60 is just a dampening constant that stops the top result from totally dominating.
For TealDR, a query like "what did sidtheitguy say about Docker" now correctly gets handled by both — the keyword librarian nails the username, the semantic librarian understands "Docker deployment discussion," and the combined ranking surfaces the right messages.
